   - **Prevention Focus:** Training, decision aids, better information systems

### B. By Action vs Cognitive Mode
| Error Type | Cognitive Basis | Observable? |
|---|---|---|
| **Slip** | Attention failure | Yes (motor) |
| **Lapse** | Memory failure | No (internal) |
| **Rule-based Mistake** | Misapplication of rule | Possibly |
| **Knowledge-based Mistake** | Lack of understanding | Possibly |

### C. By Intent (Violations)
- **Routine Violations:** Cutting corners out of habit
- **Situational Violations:** Driven by workload/pressure
- **Optimizing Violations:** For personal benefit/ thrill

## 2.2 Reason's Swiss Cheese Model (Accident Causation)

> [!NOTE]
> The Swiss Cheese Model is the **most tested framework** in KTU Module 4 questions.

This model conceptualizes system defenses as **multiple slices of Swiss cheese**, each with holes representing weaknesses. An accident occurs only when the holes in all layers align, allowing an error to penetrate.

**Four Layers of Defense:**
1. **Organizational Influences** (top management decisions, safety culture)
2. **Unsafe Supervision** (inadequate training, poor supervision)
3. **Preconditions for Unsafe Acts** (fatigue, stress, mental state)
4. **Unsafe Acts** (slips, mistakes, violations at the sharp end)

**Cumulative Failure Probability Formula:**

$$P_{accident} = P_1 \cdot P_2 \cdot P_3 \cdot P_4$$

where $P_i$ is the failure probability of the $i^{th}$ defense layer. This shows that **adding layers** geometrically reduces accident probability.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!TIP]
> This table contains all key equations, decision rules, and boundary conditions for Module 4.

| # | Formula / Principle | Application | Units / Boundary |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $HEP = \dfrac{N_{errors}}{N_{opportunities}}$ | Human Error Probability | Dimensionless, $0 \le HEP \le 1$ |
| 2 | $P_{accident} = \prod_{i=1}^{n} P_i$ | Swiss Cheese layered defense | Product of layer failures |
| 3 | $THERP = HEP \times PF$ | Technique for Human Error Rate Prediction (PF = Performance Factor) | $0 \le PF \le 10$ |
| 4 | $SLI = \dfrac{t_{error}}{t_{task}}$ | Skill Load Index (workload measure) | Ratio, $0 < SLI < 1$ |
| 5 | $P_{detection} = 1 - P_{missed}$ | Error detection probability (Poka-Yoke effectiveness) | $0 \le P_{detection} \le 1$ |
| 6 | $\text{HRA Index} = \sum (HEP_i \times W_i)$ | Weighted human reliability index | $W_i$ = task weight |
| 7 | $R_{safe} = e^{-\lambda t}$ | Reliability of human in time interval (analogous to exponential decay) | $\lambda$ = error rate per hour |
| 8 | **Poka-Yoke Principle** | Error-proofing: "Make it impossible to do wrong" | Design constraint |
| 9 | **Defensive Design Depth** | Number of independent error barriers | Integer $n \ge 2$ |
| 10 | **Hierarchy of Controls** | Elimination → Substitution → Engineering → Admin → PPE | Most to least effective |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Aviation:** Crew Resource Management (CRM), sterile cockpit rules, checklists
- **Healthcare:** Surgical checklists, double-verification of medications
- **Manufacturing:** Poka-Yoke jigs, fail-safe machine guards
- **Nuclear Industry:** SLIM-MAUD (Success Likelihood Index Methodology)
- **Automotive:** ABS, lane-departure warning, attention assist systems
- **Process Control:** Distributed Control Systems (DCS) with redundant interlocks

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Cross-Reference:** This topic directly supports Course Outcomes **CO4** (Apply ergonomic principles to prevent occupational errors) and **CO5** (Evaluate human reliability in complex systems).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Swiss Cheese Multi-Layer Defense Probability

**Given:** Four defense layers with individual failure probabilities $P_1$, $P_2$, $P_3$, $P_4$.

**Goal:** Derive the joint accident probability assuming **independent** layers.

**Step 1:** Define event $A_i$ = "Failure of $i^{th}$ defense layer."

$$P(A_i) = P_i, \quad i = 1, 2, 3, 4$$

**Step 2:** An accident occurs only when **ALL** layers fail in sequence (error propagation).

$$P_{accident} = P(A_1 \cap A_2 \cap A_3 \cap A_4)$$

**Step 3:** Apply the **multiplication rule for independent events** (a KTU standard assumption for independent defense layers).

$$\begin{aligned}
P_{accident} &= P(A_1) \cdot P(A_2) \cdot P(A_3) \cdot P(A_4) \\
&= P_1 \cdot P_2 \cdot P_3 \cdot P_4
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4:** Numerical Example (typical KTU question).
Let $P_1 = 0.1$, $P_2 = 0.2$, $P_3 = 0.3$, $P_4 = 0.4$.

$$\begin{aligned}
P_{accident} &= 0.1 \times 0.2 \times 0.3 \times 0.4 \\
&= 0.0024 \\
&= 0.24\%
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5:** Compare with single-layer failure $P_{single} = P_1 = 0.1 = 10\%$.

**Insight:** Layered defenses reduce accident probability by a factor of:

$$\text{Improvement Factor} = \frac{P_{single}}{P_{accident}} = \frac{0.1}{0.0024} \approx 41.67$$

This mathematically proves **why redundancy and multi-layer defenses are foundational to safety engineering**.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Human Error Probability (HEP) under Performance Shaping Factors (PSFs)

**Step 1:** Base HEP under standard conditions (from THERP tables):

$$HEP_{base} = f(\text{task type})$$

**Step 2:** Adjust using Performance Shaping Factors (PSFs): stress, fatigue, training, environment, equipment, complexity.

$$HEP_{actual} = HEP_{base} \cdot \prod_{j=1}^{m} PSF_j$$

**Step 3:** Common PSF numerical values (KTU expected):

| PSF | Favorable (fav) | Unfavorable (unfav) |
|---|---|---|
| Stress level | $0.8$ | $2.0$ |
| Operator experience | $0.5$ | $1.5$ |
| Time-of-day | $0.9$ | $1.8$ |
| Ergonomic fit | $0.7$ | $1.4$ |
| Task complexity | $0.6$ | $2.0$ |

**Step 4:** Worked Example.
Base $HEP_{base} = 0.01$ (1%).
Operator under moderate stress ($PSF_1 = 1.2$), high complexity ($PSF_2 = 1.8$), good training ($PSF_3 = 0.7$).

$$\begin{aligned}
HEP_{actual} &= 0.01 \times 1.2 \times 1.8 \times 0.7 \\
&= 0.01 \times 1.512 \\
&= 0.01512
\end{aligned}$$

This represents a **51.2% increase in error probability** due to unfavorable shaping factors — a common KTU 14-mark scenario.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Mapping Error Types to Prevention Strategies

> [!NOTE]
> This matrix is **high-yield** for KTU essay-type questions.

| Error Type | Cognitive Origin | Best Prevention Strategy | Design Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Slip** | Attention lapse | Forcing functions, constraints | Car key will not start in any gear except P/N |
| **Lapse** | Memory failure | Checklists, reminders, SOPs | Pre-flight checklist |
| **Rule-based Mistake** | Misapplied rule | Training, decision trees | Drug dosage calculator |
| **Knowledge-based Mistake** | Inexperience | Simulation, decision support | ATC decision aid |
| **Routine Violation** | Habit cutting | Disincentives, simplification | Time-pressure removal |
| **Situational Violation** | Workload | Workload management, breaks | Mandatory rest periods |
| **Optimizing Violation** | Personal motive | Safety culture, peer pressure | Anonymous reporting |

## 3.4 Derivation 3: Poka-Yoke (Error-Proofing) Effectiveness

**Step 1:** Define system with and without Poka-Yoke.
- Without: $P_{error} = 0.05$ (5% error rate)
- With Poka-Yoke: error is intercepted with $P_{detection} = 0.95$

**Step 2:** The effective error reaching the customer/end-user:

$$P_{error,eff} = P_{error} \times (1 - P_{detection})$$

**Step 3:** Substitute values.

$$\begin{aligned}
P_{error,eff} &= 0.05 \times (1 - 0.95) \\
&= 0.05 \times 0.05 \\
&= 0.0025 \\
&= 0.25\%
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4:** Interpretation: Poka-Yoke reduces effective error rate from **5% to 0.25%**, a **20-fold improvement** — justifying its universal adoption in lean manufacturing (Shigeo Shingo, 1986).
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Reason's Swiss Cheese Model (Block-Level Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    L4["LAYER 4 ORGANIZATIONAL INFLUENCES Top management Safety culture Resource allocation"]:::layer4
    L3["LAYER 3 UNSAFE SUPERVISION Inadequate training Poor supervision Violation tolerance"]:::layer3
    L2["LAYER 2 PRECONDITIONS FOR UNSAFE ACTS Fatigue Stress Mental state Communication"]:::layer2
    L1["LAYER 1 UNSAFE ACTS SHARP END Slips Lapses Mistakes Violations"]:::layer1
    ACC["ACCIDENT OR ADVERSE EVENT CATASTROPHIC OUTCOME"]:::accident
    TRAJ["TRAJECTORY OF ERROR PROPAGATION"]:::trajectory

    L4 -- "Latent failures descend" --> L3
    L3 -- "Local failures propagate" --> L2
    L2 -- "Direct causation" --> L1
    L1 -- "Penetration point" --> ACC
    TRAJ -. "Aligns through holes" .-> L4
    TRAJ -. "Aligns through holes" .-> L3
    TRAJ -. "Aligns through holes" .-> L2
    TRAJ -. "Aligns through holes" .-> L1

    classDef layer4 fill:#1f4e79,stroke:#0b2545,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px
    classDef layer3 fill:#2e75b6,stroke:#1f4e79,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px
    classDef layer2 fill:#9dc3e6,stroke:#2e75b6,color:#000000,stroke-width:2px
    classDef layer1 fill:#f4b183,stroke:#c55a11,color:#000000,stroke-width:2px
    classDef accident fill:#c00000,stroke:#7f0000,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:3px
    classDef trajectory fill:#ffff00,stroke:#bf9000,color:#000000,stroke-width:2px,stroke-dasharray:5 5
```

## 4.2 Hierarchical Error Prevention Framework (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["HUMAN ERROR EVENT"]:::event
    A --> B1["PERSONAL FACTORS"]:::factor
    A --> B2["TASK FACTORS"]:::factor
    A --> B3["ENVIRONMENTAL FACTORS"]:::factor
    A --> B4["ORGANIZATIONAL FACTORS"]:::factor

    B1 --> C1["Fatigue Training Experience Motivation"]
    B2 --> C2["Complexity Workload Time pressure"]
    B3 --> C3["Noise Lighting Vibration Climate"]
    B4 --> C4["Culture Communication Leadership Procedures"]

    C1 --> D["PREVENTION STRATEGIES"]:::strategy
    C2 --> D
    C3 --> D
    C4 --> D

    D --> E1["ELIMINATION"]:::lvl
    D --> E2["SUBSTITUTION"]:::lvl
    D --> E3["ENGINEERING CONTROLS"]:::lvl
    D --> E4["ADMINISTRATIVE CONTROLS"]:::lvl
    D --> E5["PPE"]:::lvl

    E1 --> F["SAFE SYSTEM PERFORMANCE"]:::outcome
    E2 --> F
    E3 --> F
    E4 --> F
    E5 --> F

    classDef event fill:#c00000,stroke:#7f0000,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px
    classDef factor fill:#2e75b6,stroke:#1f4e79,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px
    classDef strategy fill:#ffc000,stroke:#bf8f00,color:#000000,stroke-width:2px
    classDef lvl fill:#a9d18e,stroke:#548235,color:#000000,stroke-width:2px
    classDef outcome fill:#00b050,stroke:#003300,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:3px
```

## 4.3 Poka-Yoke (Error-Proofing) Functional Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S["OPERATOR INITIATES TASK"]:::start
    P["PROCESS OR ASSEMBLY STEP"]:::process
    C{"DETECTION OR CONSTRAINT CHECK"}:::check
    D["DEFECT OR ERROR PRODUCED"]:::error
    OK["TASK CONTINUES CORRECTLY"]:::correct
    STOP["PROCESS HALTED OR CORRECTED"]:::correct
    ALERT["ALARM OR FEEDBACK SIGNAL"]:::alert

    S --> P
    P --> C
    C -- "Error detected" --> STOP
    C -- "Error detected" --> ALERT
    C -- "No error" --> OK
    STOP --> P

    classDef start fill:#1f4e79,stroke:#0b2545,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px
    classDef process fill:#9dc3e6,stroke:#1f4e79,color:#000000,stroke-width:2px
    classDef check fill:#ffc000,stroke:#bf8f00,color:#000000,stroke-width:2px
    classDef error fill:#c00000,stroke:#7f0000,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px
    classDef correct fill:#00b050,stroke:#003300,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px
    classDef alert fill:#ed7d31,stroke:#843c0c,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px
```

## 4.4 Human Reliability Assessment (HRA) Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    Q1{"Is operator trained for this task?"}:::q
    Q1 -- Yes --> Q2{"Is the task under time pressure?"}:::q
    Q1 -- No --> FAIL["HIGH HEP UNACCEPTABLE RISK"]:::fail
    Q2 -- No --> Q3{"Is environment ergonomically suitable?"}:::q
    Q2 -- Yes --> STRESS["Apply stress PSF 1.5 to 2.0"]:::warn
    Q3 -- Yes --> Q4{"Are procedures well documented?"}:::q
    Q3 -- No --> ENVFIX["Redesign environment apply PSF 1.4"]:::warn
    Q4 -- Yes --> OK["ACCEPTABLE HEP WITH MONITORING"]:::pass
    Q4 -- No --> SOP["Develop SOP training"]:::warn

    classDef q fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#bf8f00,color:#000000,stroke-width:2px
    classDef pass fill:#00b050,stroke:#003300,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px
    classDef fail fill:#c00000,stroke:#7f0000,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px
    classDef warn fill:#ed7d31,stroke:#843c0c,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Define "Human Error" in the context of ergonomics and list the four main categories of human error according to Reason's classification.**

**Model Answer (10 lines):**

Human error is an unintended deviation from the operator's intended action, arising from the interaction between human limitations and system design. According to James Reason, errors are classified into four categories: **(1) Slips** — failures in execution of an intended action due to attention lapses, **(2) Lapses** — internal memory failures that are not externally observable, **(3) Mistakes** — inadequacies in the planning stage where the chosen plan fails to achieve the goal, and **(4) Violations** — deliberate deviations from prescribed procedures driven by motivation, habit, or situational factors. Each category requires a different prevention strategy — slips need design constraints, mistakes need training, and violations need cultural and organizational interventions.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Explain the concept of "Poka-Yoke" with a suitable engineering example.**

**Model Answer (10 lines):**

Poka-Yoke, a Japanese term coined by Shigeo Shingo (1986), means "mistake-proofing" or "error-proofing." It is a design philosophy that **prevents errors from occurring** or **immediately detects and corrects them** before they propagate downstream. The principle is to make it **physically impossible** or **immediately obvious** to perform an action incorrectly. **Example:** A SIM card tray in mobile phones is designed so that the card fits in only one orientation — a beveled corner prevents reversed insertion. In manufacturing, a jig that holds a part in the correct orientation automatically ensures the assembly is error-free. Poka-Yoke is a **preventive (proactive) control** in the Hierarchy of Controls and is considered the most effective engineering solution for human error reduction.

---

## Part B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each)
### (Internal Choice: Answer ANY ONE)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Adapted]**

**(a)** Explain **Reason's Swiss Cheese Model** of accident causation in detail with a suitable diagram. Discuss the four layers of defense and how latent failures differ from active failures. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A nuclear power plant operator must execute a shutdown procedure with three independent defense layers. The failure probabilities of the layers are $P_1 = 0.05$, $P_2 = 0.08$, and $P_3 = 0.10$. Calculate the **overall accident probability**. If a fourth layer with $P_4 = 0.06$ is added, by what factor does the risk improve? **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**(a) Swiss Cheese Model — Detailed Explanation:**

Reason's Swiss Cheese Model (1990) conceptualizes accident causation as the alignment of "holes" (weaknesses) in multiple defensive layers. The model identifies **four defense layers**:

1. **Organizational Influences** (top layer): Decisions by management on resource allocation, safety culture, and procedural adequacy. Failures here are **latent** — they remain dormant for long periods.

2. **Unsafe Supervision**: Failures in supervisory functions such as inadequate training, mismatch between operator skill and task, or tolerance of violations. These are also **latent failures**.

3. **Preconditions for Unsafe Acts**: Environmental and operator-state factors such as fatigue, stress, mental state, communication breakdown, and ergonomic mismatch. These create conditions for error.

4. **Unsafe Acts** (sharp end): The immediate, observable failure — slips, lapses, mistakes, and violations performed by the frontline operator. These are **active failures**.

An accident occurs when the **holes in all four layers momentarily align**, allowing the hazard trajectory to pass through. **Latent failures** are created by management decisions and lie dormant; **active failures** are the immediate triggers at the sharp end. Prevention requires **closing the holes** at every layer — through safety culture (top), supervision training (layer 2), ergonomic redesign (layer 3), and Poka-Yoke (layer 4).

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

**Step 1:** With 3 layers, accident probability:

$$\begin{aligned}
P_{accident,3} &= P_1 \cdot P_2 \cdot P_3 \\
&= 0.05 \times 0.08 \times 0.10 \\
&= 0.0004 \\
&= 0.04\%
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2:** With 4 layers added:

$$\begin{aligned}
P_{accident,4} &= 0.0004 \times 0.06 \\
&= 0.000024 \\
&= 0.0024\%
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3:** Risk improvement factor:

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Improvement Factor} &= \frac{P_{accident,3}}{P_{accident,4}} \\
&= \frac{0.0004}{0.000024} \\
&\approx 16.67
\end{aligned}$$

**Conclusion:** Adding the fourth defense layer improves safety by a factor of **~16.67**, reducing accident probability by **94%**. This is a powerful engineering justification for **defense-in-depth** strategies.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Adapted]**

**(a)** Define **Human Error Probability (HEP)**. Explain the role of **Performance Shaping Factors (PSFs)** in modifying base HEP. Discuss at least **five categories of PSFs** with examples. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** An assembly-line operator has a base $HEP = 0.02$ for a soldering task. The current PSFs are: stress $= 1.5$, poor lighting $= 1.4$, high complexity $= 1.8$, good training $= 0.7$, and adequate rest $= 0.9$. Calculate the **actual HEP**. If a Poka-Yoke system with 95% detection rate is introduced, what is the **effective error rate reaching the customer**? **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**(a) HEP and PSFs:**

**Human Error Probability (HEP)** is the quantitative measure of the likelihood that an operator fails to perform a task correctly. It is mathematically defined as:

$$HEP = \dfrac{N_{errors}}{N_{opportunities}}, \quad 0 \le HEP \le 1$$

**Performance Shaping Factors (PSFs)** are environmental, personal, task, and organizational variables that **modify the base HEP** from its standard-task value. The actual HEP is:

$$HEP_{actual} = HEP_{base} \cdot \prod_{j=1}^{m} PSF_j$$

**Five Categories of PSFs:**
1. **Internal/Personal PSFs:** Physical condition, mental state, fatigue, motivation, experience. Example: An exhausted operator may have a stress PSF of 1.8.
2. **Task PSFs:** Complexity, repetitiveness, time pressure, attention demand. Example: Multi-tasking under time pressure → PSF = 1.5–2.0.
3. **Environmental PSFs:** Lighting, noise, vibration, temperature, workspace. Example: Poor lighting → PSF = 1.4.
4. **Equipment/Interface PSFs:** Display readability, control compatibility, feedback adequacy. Example: Incompatible control layout → PSF = 1.6.
5. **Organizational PSFs:** Training quality, supervision, procedures, safety culture, communication. Example: Weak safety culture → PSF = 1.3.

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

**Step 1:** Actual HEP calculation:

$$\begin{aligned}
HEP_{actual} &= HEP_{base} \times PSF_{stress} \times PSF_{light} \times PSF_{complex} \times PSF_{train} \times PSF_{rest} \\
&= 0.02 \times 1.5 \times 1.4 \times 1.8 \times 0.7 \times 0.9 \\
&= 0.02 \times 2.3814 \\
&= 0.04763
\end{aligned}$$

So the actual HEP is **0.0476 or 4.76%**, a **138% increase** over the base HEP.

**Step 2:** Poka-Yoke with 95% detection:

$$\begin{aligned}
P_{detection} &= 0.95 \\
P_{error,eff} &= HEP_{actual} \times (1 - P_{detection}) \\
&= 0.04763 \times 0.05 \\
&= 0.002382 \\
&= 0.238\%
\end{aligned}$$

**Conclusion:** Despite poor working conditions increasing HEP to 4.76%, the Poka-Yoke reduces the **effective error rate reaching the customer to 0.24%** — a **20-fold improvement**. This demonstrates the **synergy** between PSF management and engineered error-proofing.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Do not confuse HEP with reliability.** HEP is the probability of error; reliability $R = 1 - HEP$ for a single attempt. Many students interchange them.
> 2. **PSFs multiply, they do not add.** A common error is to add PSF values. Use product form: $HEP_{actual} = HEP_{base} \cdot \prod PSF_i$.
> 3. **Swiss Cheese holes are NOT independent in real life**, but the KTU-level computation assumes independence for tractability. State this assumption explicitly in your answer.
> 4. **Do not omit units or final percentage conversion** in numerical answers — examiners deduct 1 mark.
> 5. **Use proper terminology:** "Sharp end" vs "latent end," "active failure" vs "latent failure" — wrong usage signals shallow understanding.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist (Last-Minute KTU Prep):**

- **Human Error** is systematic, not random — caused by human-system interaction.
- **Reason's 4 Error Types:** Slips (action), Lapses (memory), Mistakes (plan), Violations (intent).
- **Swiss Cheese Model:** 4 layers — Organization → Supervision → Preconditions → Unsafe Acts.
- **Accident Probability:** $P_{accident} = \prod P_i$ (independent layers, KTU assumption).
- **HEP Formula:** $HEP = N_{errors} / N_{opportunities}$.
- **Adjusted HEP:** $HEP_{actual} = HEP_{base} \cdot \prod PSF_j$ (PSFs multiply, not add).
- **Poka-Yoke:** Make it impossible to do wrong OR detect and correct immediately.
- **Poka-Yoke Effectiveness:** $P_{error,eff} = P_{error} \times (1 - P_{detection})$.
- **Hierarchy of Controls (Most → Least Effective):** Elimination → Substitution → Engineering → Administrative → PPE.
- **Prevention Pillars (3):** Design, Training, Organizational Culture.
- **Defensive Design Depth:** Adding one more independent layer geometrically reduces risk.
- **Key Authorities to Quote in Exams:** James Reason (Swiss Cheese), Shigeo Shingo (Poka-Yoke), Jens Rasmussen (SRK Framework), FAA (Crew Resource Management), WHO Surgical Safety Checklist.
- **Common Industrial Methods:** THERP, SLIM-MAUD, HEART, CREAM — name at least one in 14-mark answers.
- **SLI Formula:** $SLI = t_{error} / t_{task}$ (workload measure).
- **Reliability analog:** $R_{safe}(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$.
- **Always state independence assumption** when using the Swiss Cheese product formula.
- **Convert final numerical answers to %** for examiner-friendly presentation.
- **Use engineering examples:** Car gear interlock, USB-C, ATM card orientation, surgical timeout, aviation sterile cockpit.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Human Computer Interaction
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPET744 - ERGONOMICS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Human Computer Interaction

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Human-Computer Interaction (HCI)** is a multidisciplinary field of study situated at the intersection of **computer science, cognitive psychology, design, and human factors engineering** that focuses on the design, evaluation, and implementation of interactive computing systems for human use, along with the study of the major phenomena surrounding them.

The **Association for Computing Machinery (ACM)** defines HCI as *"a discipline concerned with the design, evaluation and implementation of interactive computing systems for human use and with the study of major phenomena surrounding them."*

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core HCI Triad (The 3 Pillars):**
> 1. **The Human** — User's cognitive, perceptual, and motor capabilities.
> 2. **The Computer** — Hardware, software, input/output modalities.
> 3. **The Interaction** — The dialogue, feedback, and task flow between them.

**Usability** is the primary quality attribute of HCI. According to **Nielsen (1993)**, a system has high usability when it is:
- **Learnable**
- **Efficient**
- **Memorable**
- **Error-tolerant** (low error rate)
- **Satisfying** (subjectively pleasant)

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition

Imagine a tourist visiting a foreign country. The **human** is the tourist, the **computer** is the city's infrastructure (signs, transport, language), and the **interaction** is the journey from the airport to the hotel.

- If the signs are unclear, the language is alien, and transport is confusing — the tourist gets lost, frustrated, and exhausted. This is **POOR HCI**.
- If the signs use universal symbols, the language is translated, and the transport is intuitive — the tourist reaches the hotel efficiently and feels satisfied. This is **GOOD HCI**.

> [!NOTE]
> HCI is essentially the **"translation layer"** that makes technology feel invisible, intuitive, and natural to the human mind. Just as a well-designed door handle "tells" you whether to push or pull, a well-designed interface tells the user what to do next.

### 1.3 Why HCI Matters in Ergonomics

In the KTU 2024 Ergonomics syllabus, HCI is a critical module because it extends classical human-factors principles (originally applied to physical tools, cockpits, and machinery) into the **digital and cognitive workspace**. The goal is to **minimize cognitive load**, **prevent mental fatigue**, and **maximize productivity** by aligning the system with human mental models.

### 1.4 The Three-Way Symmetry of HCI (Dix et al.)

| Dimension | Focus | Example in Ergonomics |
|---|---|---|
| Human | Who is the user? | Operator skill, fatigue level, training |
| Computer | What is the technology? | Display, keyboard, voice interface |
| Interaction | How do they communicate? | Menus, gestures, feedback |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

### 2.1 The Human Component (Cognitive Architecture)

The human mind is the ultimate processor in any HCI system. Designers must understand:

#### A. Human Cognition
- **Perception**: Sensory input via vision, hearing, touch. Approx. **8.0 million bits/sec** enter the brain, but conscious processing is only **~50 bits/sec**.
- **Memory**:
  - *Sensory Memory*: < 1 second buffer (iconic/echonic).
  - *Short-Term Memory (STM) / Working Memory*: **7 ± 2 chunks** (Miller's Law) for ~15–30 seconds.
  - *Long-Term Memory (LTM)*: Unlimited capacity, organized semantically.
- **Attention**: Selective focus; only one main task can dominate at a time.
- **Problem Solving & Reasoning**: Heuristic vs. algorithmic.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Miller's Law (1956):** The average human can hold **7 ± 2 chunks** of information in short-term memory. Implication for HCI: Menus, navigation tabs, and grouped options should respect this limit (e.g., 5–9 items max).

#### B. The Motor System
- **Fitts' Law** governs pointing. See formula sheet.
- Reaction time for a simple stimulus: **~250 ms** (mental processing).
- Reaction time for a choice reaction: **~500 ms** (selection from options).

#### C. Human Errors
- **Slips**: Correct intention, wrong execution (motor inattention).
- **Mistakes**: Wrong intention, wrong mental model.
- Design must **prevent**, **detect**, and **recover** from both.

### 2.2 The Computer Component (System Constraints)

- **Input Devices**: Keyboard, mouse, touchpad, touchscreen, microphone (speech), eye-tracker, motion sensors.
- **Output Devices**: Monitor (visual), speaker (auditory), haptic (tactile), printer.
- **Memory & Bandwidth**: 8 GB RAM, 60–240 Hz refresh rate display, etc.
- **Operating System & Platform**: Windows, macOS, Linux, Android, iOS.

### 2.3 The Interaction Component (Design Principles)

#### Don Norman's Seven Principles of Design (Visibility, Feedback, etc.):
1. **Visibility**: System status must be visible.
2. **Feedback**: User must receive clear confirmation of actions.
3. **Affordance**: A button should *look* pressable.
4. **Mapping**: Controls should map naturally to their effects.
5. **Consistency**: Similar operations should produce similar results.
6. **Constraint**: Restrict possible actions to prevent errors.
7. **Error Tolerance**: Provide graceful recovery from mistakes.

#### Nielsen's 10 Usability Heuristics (1994):
1. Visibility of system status
2. Match between system and the real world
3. User control and freedom
4. Consistency and standards
5. Error prevention
6. Recognition rather than recall
7. Flexibility and efficiency of use
8. Aesthetic and minimalist design
9. Help users recognize, diagnose, and recover from errors
10. Help and documentation

### 2.4 Models of Interaction

| Model | Origin | Core Idea |
|---|---|---|
| **Norman's Seven Stages of Action** (1986) | Don Norman | User forms goal → executes → perceives → interprets → evaluates |
| **GOMS Model** | Card, Moran, Newell | Goals, Operators, Methods, Selection rules — predicts task time |
| **Fitts' Law** | Paul Fitts (1954) | Mathematical model of pointing time vs. distance & target size |
| **Hick's Law** | William Hick (1952) | Decision time = $a + b \cdot \log_2(n+1)$ for *n* choices |
| **Mental Model** | Craik (1943) | User's internal conceptual model of system behaviour |

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> All quantities are in **seconds, meters, and bits**. Pay strict attention to units — KTU examiners frequently test this.

| S.No | Law / Formula | Mathematical Form | Variables & Units | Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Fitts' Law** (Pointing Time) | $T = a + b \cdot \log_2\!\left(\dfrac{D}{W} + 1\right)$ | $T$ = movement time (s); $D$ = distance to target (m); $W$ = target width (m); $a, b$ = empirically derived constants (typically $a \approx 0.05$ s, $b \approx 0.10$ s/bit) | Designing button sizes, menu spacing, touchscreen target dimensions |
| 2 | **Index of Difficulty (ID)** | $ID = \log_2\!\left(\dfrac{D}{W} + 1\right)$ | Dimensionless, in **bits** | Quantifies task difficulty |
| 3 | **Throughput (TP)** | $TP = \dfrac{ID}{T}$ | Measured in **bits/second** | Evaluates device efficiency (typical mouse: 4–10 bps) |
| 4 | **Hick's Law** (Reaction Time) | $T = a + b \cdot \log_2(n+1)$ | $T$ = reaction time (s); $n$ = number of equally likely choices; $a, b$ = constants | Limiting menu options to reduce decision time |
| 5 | **Miller's Law** | STM capacity $= 7 \pm 2$ | Chunks of information | Grouping information, chunking UI items |
| 6 | **GOMS — Keystroke-Level Model (KLM)** | $T_{execute} = T_k + T_p + T_h + T_d + T_m + T_r$ | $T_k$ = keystrokes, $T_p$ = pointing, $T_h$ = homing, $T_d$ = drawing, $T_m$ = mental prep, $T_r$ = response | Predicting expert task time |
| 7 | **Stevens' Power Law** | $\psi = k \cdot I^{\,n}$ | $\psi$ = perceived magnitude, $I$ = physical intensity, $k, n$ = constants | Designing visual/auditory feedback |
| 8 | **KLM — Card, Moran, Newell Constants** | $T_k = 0.20$ s, $T_p = 1.10$ s, $T_m = 1.35$ s, $T_h = 0.40$ s, $T_d = 0.40$ s/element, $T_r = 0.40$ s | All in **seconds** | Standard KLM execution times |

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

These formulas are the **backbone of UI/UX design**:
- **Fitts' Law** is used by Apple, Microsoft, and Google to size **touch targets** on iOS, Android, and Windows (minimum 44×44 px on iOS, 48 dp on Android).
- **Hick's Law** dictates why **hamburger menus** collapse choices and why **search bars** outperform long navigation trees.
- **GOMS** is used in safety-critical systems (avionics, nuclear control rooms) to predict operator response time.
- **Stevens' Power Law** governs the design of progress bars, sound effects, and brightness curves.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For KTU numerical problems, **Fitts' Law is the most frequently tested formula**. Always compute $\log_2$ carefully. Remember that $\log_2(x) = \dfrac{\ln(x)}{\ln(2)} = \dfrac{\log_{10}(x)}{\log_{10}(2)}$.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of Fitts' Law and Worked Examples

#### Derivation 3.1.1 — Standard Fitts' Law Equation

Fitts' Law is a **Shannon-Hartley inspired** information-theoretic model. Paul Fitts (1954) reasoned that the difficulty of a pointing task is analogous to the **information capacity** of a communication channel.

The **Shannon-Hartley theorem** states the maximum information transmitted over a channel of bandwidth $B$ in time $T$ is:

$$C = B \cdot T \cdot \log_2\!\left(1 + \dfrac{S}{N}\right)$$

Fitts substituted the **spatial signal-to-noise ratio** in place of $\dfrac{S}{N}$:

$$\dfrac{D}{W} \;\longrightarrow\; \dfrac{S}{N}$$

where:
- $D$ = amplitude of movement (signal) → analogous to signal power.
- $W$ = tolerance or target width (noise) → analogous to noise.

Substituting into the Shannon form yields the **Index of Difficulty (ID)**:

$$ID = \log_2\!\left(\dfrac{D}{W} + 1\right) \quad \text{[in bits]}$$

Movement time is then modeled as linearly proportional to the information load:

$$T = a + b \cdot ID$$

Combining both expressions produces the canonical Fitts' Law:

$$T = a + b \cdot \log_2\!\left(\dfrac{D}{W} + 1\right)$$

Where:
- $a$ = start/stop time of the limb (intercept), typically $0.05$ to $0.20$ seconds.
- $b$ = limb acceleration/deceleration coefficient, typically $0.10$ to $0.20$ seconds per bit.
- The $+$ 1 inside the logarithm prevents negative infinity when $D = 0$.

#### Derivation 3.1.2 — Throughput Derivation

Throughput measures the **rate at which information is transmitted** during a motor act. By definition, throughput is information transmitted per unit time:

$$TP = \dfrac{ID}{T}$$

Substituting $T = a + b \cdot ID$, the throughput across multiple trials is computed as the **slope of the regression** of $ID$ vs. $T$:

$$TP = \dfrac{1}{b} \quad \text{[bits/second]}$$

This is why $b$ in Fitts' Law is often called the *reciprocal of throughput*.

---

### 3.2 KTU-Style Numerical Problem (Fully Solved)

> **Problem (KTU ESE Pattern):**
> A user moves the mouse cursor a distance of $D = 20$ cm to click a button of width $W = 2$ cm. Using Fitts' Law with empirical constants $a = 0.05$ s and $b = 0.10$ s/bit, calculate:
> (a) Index of Difficulty (ID)
> (b) Movement Time (T)
> (c) Throughput (TP)

#### Solution — Step-by-Step

**Step 1:** Identify knowns.
- $D = 20$ cm, $W = 2$ cm, $a = 0.05$ s, $b = 0.10$ s/bit.

**Step 2:** Compute the **ratio** $D/W$:

$$\dfrac{D}{W} = \dfrac{20 \text{ cm}}{2 \text{ cm}} = 10$$

**Step 3:** Add 1 to prevent singularity:

$$\dfrac{D}{W} + 1 = 10 + 1 = 11$$

**Step 4:** Compute the **base-2 logarithm**:

$$\log_2(11) = \dfrac{\ln(11)}{\ln(2)} = \dfrac{2.3979}{0.6931} \approx 3.4594 \text{ bits}$$

**Step 5:** Answer to (a) — Index of Difficulty:

$$ID = 3.4594 \text{ bits}$$

**Step 6:** Answer to (b) — Movement Time using Fitts' Law:

$$T = a + b \cdot ID$$

$$T = 0.05 + (0.10) \cdot (3.4594)$$

$$T = 0.05 + 0.34594$$

$$T \approx 0.396 \text{ seconds}$$

**Step 7:** Answer to (c) — Throughput:

$$TP = \dfrac{ID}{T} = \dfrac{3.4594}{0.396} \approx 8.74 \text{ bits/second}$$

> [!NOTE]
> A throughput of **~8.74 bps** is realistic for a mouse-based pointing task. Typical reported values range from **4–10 bps**.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Applying HCI Models to Ergonomic Problems

| S.No | Engineering Scenario | Best-Fit HCI Model | Reason for Selection |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Designing touchscreen button size for industrial control panel | **Fitts' Law** | Determines minimum target size for given accuracy & distance |
| 2 | Deciding number of options in an emergency menu | **Hick's Law** | Decision time grows logarithmically with choices; minimize $n$ |
| 3 | Predicting pilot reaction time in cockpit | **GOMS / KLM** | Decomposes task into primitive operators with known times |
| 4 | Grouping alarm indicators on a dashboard | **Miller's Law** | STM holds 7 ± 2 chunks; cluster items into 5–9 groups |
| 5 | Designing haptic feedback for VR training simulator | **Stevens' Power Law** | Predicts perceived intensity from physical stimulus |
| 6 | Evaluating "Undo" button placement | **Norman's 7 Stages of Action** | "User control and freedom" principle in Nielsen heuristics |
| 7 | Cognitive load of surgeon using robotic console | **Mental Model & STM limits** | Prevents information overload |
| 8 | Reducing error rate in a medical infusion pump | **Reason's Swiss Cheese Model** | Multiple barriers to error propagation |

### 3.4 Comparative Derivation Table — Hick's Law vs. Fitts' Law

| Parameter | Fitts' Law (Motor) | Hick's Law (Cognitive) |
|---|---|---|
| Domain | Physical movement | Cognitive choice |
| Independent Variable | Distance $D$, Width $W$ | Number of choices $n$ |
| Output | Movement time $T$ | Reaction time $T$ |
| Equation | $T = a + b \cdot \log_2\!\left(\dfrac{D}{W} + 1\right)$ | $T = a + b \cdot \log_2(n+1)$ |
| Underlying Theory | Shannon-Hartley (signal/noise) | Information theory (bits of choice) |
| KTU Use Case | Button sizing, touch targets | Menu depth, list length |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

### 4.1 Norman's Seven Stages of Action (Execution vs. Evaluation Cycle)

This is the canonical HCI framework. It splits the human-system loop into **Execution** (Goal → World) and **Evaluation** (World → Goal).

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    node1["User Goal Formation"]
    node2["Intention Specification"]
    node3["Action Sequence"]
    node4["Execution of Action"]
    node5{"Perception of System State"}
    node6["Interpretation of State"]
    node7["Evaluation of Outcome"}

    node1 --> node2
    node2 --> node3
    node3 --> node4
    node4 --> node5
    node5 --> node6
    node6 --> node7
    node7 -.Feedback.-> node1

    node4 -.-> node5

    nodeA["GULF OF EXECUTION"]:::exec
    nodeB["GULF OF EVALUATION"]:::eval

    nodeA -.- node2
    nodeA -.- node3
    nodeA -.- node4
    nodeB -.- node5
    nodeB -.- node6

    classDef exec fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,color:#000
    classDef eval fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#000,color:#000
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Gulf of Execution** = gap between user's intention and the actions the system allows. **Gulf of Evaluation** = gap between system state and user's understanding of it. Good HCI design **minimizes both gulfs**.

### 4.2 HCI Design Lifecycle (Star Model by Hartson & Pyla)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    phase1["1. REQUIREMENTS ANALYSIS"]
    phase2["2. DESIGN CONCEPT"]
    phase3["3. PROTOTYPING"]
    phase4["4. IMPLEMENTATION"]
    phase5["5. EVALUATION & TESTING"]

    phase1 --> phase2
    phase2 --> phase3
    phase3 --> phase4
    phase4 --> phase5
    phase5 -.Iterate Back.-> phase1

    detailA["User Research, Task Analysis, Personas"]:::detail
    detailB["Sketches, Wireframes, Affinity Diagrams"]:::detail
    detailC["Low-Fidelity and High-Fidelity Mockups"]:::detail
    detailD["Code Development and Integration"]:::detail
    detailE["Usability Testing, Heuristic Evaluation, A/B Testing"]:::detail

    phase1 --- detailA
    phase2 --- detailB
    phase3 --- detailC
    phase4 --- detailD
    phase5 --- detailE

    classDef detail fill:#E0FFE0,stroke:#006400,color:#000
```

### 4.3 GOMS Model Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    gomsG["GOAL: Top-level user objective"]
    gomsM["METHOD: Sequence of steps to achieve goal"]
    gomsO["OPERATORS: Atomic actions keystroke, point, click"]
    gomsS["SELECTION RULES: Choose between competing methods"]

    gomsG --> gomsM
    gomsM --> gomsO
    gomsG --> gomsS
    gomsS -.Chooses.-> gomsM

    style gomsG fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#000
    style gomsM fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#000
    style gomsO fill:#98FB98,stroke:#000
    style gomsS fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#000
```

### 4.4 Block-Level Functional Architecture of an Interactive System

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    userA["HUMAN USER"]
    input1["INPUT CHANNELS: Keyboard, Mouse, Touch, Voice"]
    proc1["PROCESSING UNIT: OS, Application Logic, Memory"]
    output1["OUTPUT CHANNELS: Display, Speaker, Haptic Device"]
    feedback1["FEEDBACK LOOP: System Response"]

    userA -->|"Perceives Output"| feedback1
    feedback1 --> output1
    output1 --> userA

    userA -->|"Issues Command"| input1
    input1 --> proc1
    proc1 -->|"Computation & State Change"| output1

    constraintA["CONSTRAINTS: Time, Accuracy, Cognitive Load"]:::cstr
    proc1 -.- constraintA

    classDef cstr fill:#FFE4E1,stroke:#8B0000,color:#000
```

### 4.5 Usability Heuristics — Decision Tree Mapping (Block Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA["Evaluate Interface"] --> q1{"Is System Status Visible?"}
    q1 -- No --> fix1["Add progress indicators and status bar"]
    q1 -- Yes --> q2{"Does language match user mental model?"}
    q2 -- No --> fix2["Use familiar icons and plain language"]
    q2 -- Yes --> q3{"Does it allow undo and cancel?"}
    q3 -- No --> fix3["Provide back button and undo stack"]
    q3 -- Yes --> q4{"Are similar tasks handled consistently?"}
    q4 -- No --> fix4["Standardize UI elements"]
    q4 -- Yes --> q5{"Does it prevent errors proactively?"}
    q5 -- No --> fix5["Add confirmations and constraints"]
    q5 -- Yes --> q6{"Recognition over Recall?"}
    q6 -- No --> fix6["Show options not requiring memory"]
    q6 -- Yes --> done["Heuristic Compliance Achieved"]

    style startA fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000
    style done fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### Question 1: Define Human-Computer Interaction (HCI). List any four usability attributes. [3 Marks]
**[Model Answer]**:
**Human-Computer Interaction (HCI)** is the multidisciplinary field concerned with the design, evaluation, and implementation of interactive computing systems for human use, and the study of phenomena surrounding them (per ACM).

The four usability attributes as per **Nielsen (1993)** are:
1. **Learnability** — How easy it is for a user to accomplish a task the first time.
2. **Efficiency** — How quickly tasks can be performed once learned.
3. **Memorability** — How easily a user can re-establish proficiency after a period of non-use.
4. **Errors** — The frequency of errors, severity, and ease of recovery.

*(Any 4 of: Learnability, Efficiency, Memorability, Errors, Satisfaction)*

#### Question 2: State Fitts' Law. What does its throughput measure? [3 Marks]
**[Model Answer]**:
**Fitts' Law** states that the time $T$ required to move a pointer to a target is a logarithmic function of the distance $D$ to the target and the target width $W$:

$$T = a + b \cdot \log_2\!\left(\dfrac{D}{W} + 1\right)$$

The **Index of Difficulty (ID)** is:

$$ID = \log_2\!\left(\dfrac{D}{W} + 1\right) \quad \text{(in bits)}$$

**Throughput (TP)** measures the **rate of information transfer** during the pointing task:

$$TP = \dfrac{ID}{T} \quad \text{(in bits/second)}$$

Throughput is device-independent and is used to compare the efficiency of different input devices (e.g., mouse vs. touchscreen).

---

### 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice)

> **KTU 2024 Pattern Note:** Each Part B question offers internal choice. Below, Question A and Question B are two fully independent alternatives on HCI.

#### ⭐ QUESTION A (14 Marks)

##### (a) Explain Norman's Seven Stages of Action with a neat diagram. Discuss the significance of the **Gulf of Execution** and the **Gulf of Evaluation** in interface design. [7 Marks]

**[Model Answer]**:

Don Norman's **Seven Stages of Action** (1986) is a foundational HCI model that describes how a user accomplishes a goal through an interactive system. The stages are split into two phases:

**A. Execution Phase (Going from User to System):**
1. **Goal Formation** — The user formulates a high-level objective (e.g., "I want to print this document").
2. **Intention Specification** — The user decides *what* to do (e.g., "I will use the File menu").
3. **Action Sequence** — The user plans *how* to do it (e.g., "Click File → Print → OK").
4. **Execution** — The user actually performs the action on the system.

**B. Evaluation Phase (Going from System to User):**
5. **Perception of System State** — The user sees the result (e.g., the printer dialog appears).
6. **Interpretation of State** — The user makes sense of what is displayed.
7. **Evaluation of Outcome** — The user judges whether the goal was achieved.

**Gulf of Execution**: The gap between the user's *intention* and the *actions the system permits*. A good interface narrows this gulf by offering clear, learnable, and well-mapped controls (e.g., a "print" icon that visually suggests printing).

**Gulf of Evaluation**: The gap between the *system's actual state* and the user's *understanding* of that state. A good interface narrows this gulf by providing immediate, visible, and intuitive feedback (e.g., a printer icon appearing in the system tray to confirm the print job started).

**Significance for Interface Design**:
- Designers must ensure **clear mapping** (intuitive controls) to reduce the Gulf of Execution.
- Designers must ensure **continuous, visible feedback** to reduce the Gulf of Evaluation.
- Together, these reduce user errors, training time, and cognitive load.

*Refer to the Mermaid diagram in Section 4.1 for the visual cycle.*

##### (b) Define Usability. Explain Nielsen's 10 Usability Heuristics for User Interface Design. [7 Marks]

**[Model Answer]**:

**Usability** is a quality attribute that assesses how easy a user interface is to use. It encompasses **learnability, efficiency, memorability, error tolerance, and satisfaction** (Nielsen, 1993).

**Nielsen's 10 Usability Heuristics** (1994):

| No. | Heuristic | Meaning in HCI |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Visibility of System Status** | Always keep the user informed through feedback (e.g., progress bar). |
| 2 | **Match between System and Real World** | Use familiar language, conventions, and units. |
| 3 | **User Control and Freedom** | Provide undo, redo, cancel, and exit options. |
| 4 | **Consistency and Standards** | Same action = same result across the system. |
| 5 | **Error Prevention** | Design to prevent errors from occurring. |
| 6 | **Recognition rather than Recall** | Show options instead of asking users to remember them. |
| 7 | **Flexibility and Efficiency of Use** | Provide shortcuts for expert users. |
| 8 | **Aesthetic and Minimalist Design** | Avoid irrelevant information. |
| 9 | **Help Users Recognize, Diagnose, and Recover from Errors** | Use plain language error messages with solutions. |
| 10 | **Help and Documentation** | Provide easy-to-search, task-oriented help. |

These heuristics are the **gold standard for heuristic evaluation**, a low-cost usability inspection method widely used in industry.

---

#### ⭐ QUESTION B (14 Marks)

##### (a) What is Fitts' Law? State and derive the equation. Compute the movement time for $D = 15$ cm, $W = 1$ cm, $a = 0.05$ s, $b = 0.12$ s/bit. [7 Marks]

**[Model Answer]**:

**Fitts' Law** (Paul Fitts, 1954) is a predictive model of human motor behavior that quantifies the relationship between movement time, distance to a target, and target size.

**Derivation**:
Drawing from Shannon-Hartley information theory, Fitts equated the spatial signal-to-noise ratio of a motor task to the channel capacity:

$$ID = \log_2\!\left(\dfrac{D}{W} + 1\right) \quad \text{[bits]}$$

Movement time is linearly proportional to information:

$$T = a + b \cdot ID$$

Combining:

$$\boxed{\,T = a + b \cdot \log_2\!\left(\dfrac{D}{W} + 1\right)\,}$$

**Numerical Solution**:
- Given: $D = 15$ cm, $W = 1$ cm, $a = 0.05$ s, $b = 0.12$ s/bit.
- Step 1: $D/W = 15/1 = 15$.
- Step 2: $D/W + 1 = 16$.
- Step 3: $\log_2(16) = 4$ bits.
- Step 4: $T = 0.05 + (0.12)(4) = 0.05 + 0.48 = \mathbf{0.53 \text{ seconds}}$.

##### (b) Explain the GOMS Model in detail. Write the KLM (Keystroke-Level Model) equation and list the standard time constants. [7 Marks]

**[Model Answer]**:

The **GOMS Model** (Card, Moran, Newell, 1983) is a cognitive, hierarchical model that predicts user performance by decomposing a task into goals and methods.

**Four Components**:
- **G — Goals**: The user's high-level objective (e.g., "Save the file").
- **O — Operators**: Atomic actions (e.g., keystroke, point, click).
- **M — Methods**: The procedure to achieve a goal (e.g., "Click File → Save").
- **S — Selection Rules**: Logic to choose between competing methods (e.g., "If file is new, use 'Save As'").

**KLM (Keystroke-Level Model)** is the most applied GOMS variant. Total task time is the sum of operator times:

$$T_{execute} = T_k + T_p + T_h + T_d + T_m + T_r$$

**Standard KLM Constants** (Card, Moran, Newell, 1980):

| Symbol | Operator | Time (seconds) |
|---|---|---|
| $T_k$ | Keystroke | 0.20 |
| $T_p$ | Pointing (with mouse) | 1.10 |
| $T_h$ | Homing (hand to device) | 0.40 |
| $T_d$ | Drawing (per element) | 0.40 |
| $T_m$ | Mental preparation | 1.35 |
| $T_r$ | System response | 0.40 |

**Use Case**: KLM is used in the design of safety-critical interfaces (e.g., cockpit displays) to predict expert operator times before deployment.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting the "+1" inside the log** of Fitts' Law — examiners frequently deduct 1 mark for $\log_2(D/W)$ instead of $\log_2(D/W + 1)$.
> 2. **Unit Mismatch** — Mixing cm and mm in Fitts' Law gives wrong ID; convert to consistent units first.
> 3. **Confusing Hick's Law (cognitive) with Fitts' Law (motor)** — both are logarithmic but apply to different stages.
> 4. **Confusing Miller's Law (7 ± 2 chunks) with short-term memory duration** — it is about *capacity*, not time.
> 5. **Skipping the diagram in Norman's stages question** — always draw the cycle; loss of 1–2 marks for missing diagram.
> 6. **In GOMS, omitting the 'S' (Selection Rules)** — many students write only GOM; full form is **GOMS**.

---

### 5.3 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST

- [x] **HCI Definition** — Multidisciplinary design of interactive systems.
- [x] **Three Pillars** — Human, Computer, Interaction.
- [x] **Usability Attributes (Nielsen 1993)** — Learnable, Efficient, Memorable, Error-tolerant, Satisfying.
- [x] **Miller's Law** — STM = **7 ± 2 chunks**.
- [x] **Fitts' Law** — $T = a + b \cdot \log_2(D/W + 1)$; ID in bits; TP in bps.
- [x] **Hick's Law** — $T = a + b \cdot \log_2(n+1)$.
- [x] **Norman's 7 Stages** — Goal → Intention → Action → Execution → Perception → Interpretation → Evaluation.
- [x] **Gulf of Execution** — User intention vs. system action.
- [x] **Gulf of Evaluation** — System state vs. user understanding.
- [x] **GOMS** — Goals, Operators, Methods, Selection rules.
- [x] **KLM Constants** — $T_k = 0.20$, $T_p = 1.10$, $T_m = 1.35$, $T_h = 0.40$, $T_d = 0.40$, $T_r = 0.40$ (all in seconds).
- [x] **Stevens' Power Law** — $\psi = k \cdot I^n$.
- [x] **Nielsen's 10 Heuristics** — Especially 1, 2, 4, 5, 6 for exam.
- [x] **Touch target standards** — iOS: 44×44 px, Android: 48 dp, Windows: 40×40 px.
- [x] **Slip vs. Mistake** — Slip = motor, Mistake = cognitive.
- [x] **HCI Lifecycle** — Requirements → Concept → Prototype → Implement → Evaluate.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Human Centred Design Process for Interactive Systems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPET744 - ERGONOMICS
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Human Centred Design Process for Interactive Systems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (ISO 9241-210)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Human-Centred Design (HCD)** is defined by the international standard **ISO 9241-210:2019** as an *approach to interactive systems development that aims to make interactive systems more usable by focusing on the use of the system and applying human factors/ergonomics and usability knowledge and techniques.*

In the KTU 2024 scheme framework for Ergonomics, Human-Centred Design is treated as a **structured, iterative engineering methodology** rather than a casual "design philosophy." It explicitly ties user research, task analysis, prototyping, and evaluation into a closed-loop development cycle.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — "The Tailor Who Measures Twice"**
>
> Imagine ordering a custom suit. A *non-HCD tailor* would cut fabric based on his own taste and assumptions about the customer. The result: a beautiful-looking suit that does not fit.
>
> An **HCD tailor** would, *before cutting*:
> 1. **Measure** your body (Understand context of use)
> 2. **Discuss** your lifestyle, occupation, and climate (Specify user requirements)
> 3. **Draft a pattern** and try it on a mannequin first, then on you (Produce design solutions + Prototype)
> 4. **Iterate** after every fitting (Evaluate and refine)
>
> The Interactive System = the suit. The User = the customer. The HCD process ensures the final product *fits the human reality*, not the designer's fantasy.

---

## 1.3 Why It Matters — Standard Metrics

The KTU syllabus highlights that the effectiveness of an interactive system is judged against four pillars laid down in **ISO 9241-11**:

| Pillar | Definition | Engineering Implication |
|---|---|---|
| **Effectiveness** | Accuracy & completeness with which users achieve goals | Task completion rate (%) |
| **Efficiency** | Resources expended in relation to effectiveness | Time-on-task (seconds) |
| **Satisfaction** | Freedom from discomfort, positive attitudes | SUS Score (0–100) |
| **Context of Use** | Users, tasks, equipment, physical/social environment | Defined operational envelope |

> [!TIP]
> **Mnemonic — "E²S + C"**: **E**ffectiveness, **E**fficiency, **S**atisfaction, in a defined **C**ontext. This is a guaranteed 3-mark short-answer in KTU examinations.

---

## 1.4 Scope Within Module 4

Within Module 4, this topic is the **backbone** that supports later sub-topics such as usability heuristics, prototyping, and evaluation methods. Every other sub-topic (e.g., user research techniques, prototyping fidelity levels, usability testing) feeds into one of the four pillars of the HCD process.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Four Pillars of the HCD Process

The ISO 9241-210 standard prescribes **four core activities** that are performed iteratively (not sequentially) throughout the design life-cycle:

### Activity 1 — Plan the HCD Process
- Identify the **users**, **goals**, **tasks**, and **constraints**.
- Define the **timing, scope, methods, and resources** for each HCD activity.
- Produce a *Human-Centred Design Plan* — a living document.

### Activity 2 — Understand and Specify the Context of Use
- Identify users by **characteristics** (age, experience, motivation, physical/cognitive abilities).
- Identify **goals and tasks** the system must support.
- Identify the **environment** of use (lighting, noise, vibration, social setting).

### Activity 3 — Specify the User Requirements
- Translate context-of-use findings into **concrete, measurable requirements**.
- Each requirement should map to a usability goal expressed in **Effectiveness / Efficiency / Satisfaction** terms.

### Activity 4 — Produce Design Solutions
- Generate **alternative design concepts** (sketches, wireframes, prototypes).
- Move from **low-fidelity → high-fidelity** prototypes iteratively.

### (The Loop) Activity 5 — Evaluate the Designs Against Requirements
- Conduct **formative evaluation** during design and **summative evaluation** at release.
- Use **usability testing, heuristic evaluation, walkthroughs, field studies**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Iteration Matters:** Real users behave *differently* from assumptions. Each cycle of "evaluate → refine" closes the gap between *intended* and *actual* use.

---

## 2.2 The Six Guiding Principles of HCD (ISO 9241-210)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> These principles are **frequently asked as 7–10 mark questions**. Memorise them in order.

1. **Explicit Understanding of Users, Tasks, and Environments** — design decisions are grounded in empirical evidence, not assumptions.
2. **Users Are Involved Throughout Design and Development** — active participation, not passive observation.
3. **Design Is Driven and Refined by User-Centred Evaluation** — every iteration produces measurable feedback.
4. **Design Addresses the Whole User Experience** — beyond task completion to satisfaction, emotion, value.
5. **Design Team Includes Multidisciplinary Skills and Perspectives** — ergonomists, developers, domain experts, users.
6. **The Process Is Iterative** — until usability goals are satisfied.

---

## 2.3 Benefits of Human-Centred Design (Examinable)

| Benefit | Mechanism | Typical Reported Magnitude |
|---|---|---|
| Increased **Productivity** | Fewer errors, faster task completion | **10–30%** efficiency gain |
| Increased **User Satisfaction** | Better fit to user mental models | SUS score gains of **15–25** points |
| **Reduced Training & Support Costs** | Intuitive UI | Savings of **30–70%** in helpdesk calls |
| Reduced **Development Time & Cost** | Late defects are expensive to fix | **100×** cost multiplier per defect stage |
| **Reduced Risk** | Compliance with accessibility law | Legal liability mitigation |
| Improved **Accessibility** | Inclusive design for diverse users | Broader market reach |

---

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Usability Metrics)

> [!WARNING]
> **Anti-Hallucination Protocol:** These formulas are taken from ISO 9241-11 and Nielsen's classical usability literature, both taught under KTU PEPET744.

$$
E_{\text{effectiveness}} \;=\; \frac{\text{Tasks Completed Successfully}}{\text{Tasks Attempted}} \times 100\%
$$

$$
E_{\text{efficiency}} \;=\; \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} \text{Time}_i}{\sum_{i=1}^{n} \text{Successful Tasks}_i} \quad \text{[seconds per task]}
$$

$$
\text{SUS Score} \;=\; \sum_{j=1}^{10} \text{adjusted item score}_j \times 2.5 \quad \text{[scale: 0–100]}
$$

$$
\text{Relative Efficiency Improvement} \;=\; \frac{E_{\text{after}} \;-\; E_{\text{before}}}{E_{\text{before}}} \times 100\%
$$

> Where *i* indexes tasks, *j* indexes the 10 System Usability Scale items, and 2.5 is the standard normalisation constant.

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

In professional practice (e.g., medical devices, automotive HMIs, avionics cockpits), the HCD process is **regulatory-mandated**. Standards such as **IEC 62366-1** (medical devices) and **DO-178C / DO-330** (avionics) explicitly require usability engineering processes that mirror ISO 9241-210. For a KTU student, mastery of this topic directly translates to **Human Factors Engineering roles** in automotive (e.g., Tata Elxsi, Continental), medical (e.g., GE Healthcare), and product design firms.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Walkthrough — Worked Case Study

## 3.1 Case Study Setup

> **Scenario:** You are the ergonomist for *MedTech Kerala Pvt. Ltd.* The company wants to design a **portable pulse oximeter** for rural ASHA workers. You are required to apply the **Human-Centred Design process** to it.

This worked example mirrors the typical 14-mark analytical question that KTU examiners set in Part B.

---

### STEP 1 — Plan the HCD Process (Activity 1)

**Conversion Logic:** First, scope the project. Identify what we know and what we do not know.

| Project Dimension | Documented Value |
|---|---|
| Target user group | ASHA workers (rural, semi-literate, gloves at night) |
| Use environment | Low light, intermittent power, dusty, monsoon humidity |
| Core task | Measure SpO₂ and pulse within 10 s |
| Budget | ₹15 Lakh prototype phase |
| Timeline | 6 months, 3 design–evaluate cycles |
| Methods | Contextual inquiry, task analysis, heuristic eval, field test |

**Why this step matters:** Without this plan, subsequent user research becomes *directionless*. The plan is the *contract* between designers and stakeholders.

---

### STEP 2 — Understand & Specify the Context of Use (Activity 2)

**Conversion Logic:** Empirically gather data, not from assumptions.

#### 2a — User Characteristics

$$
U_{\text{profile}} \;=\; \{\, \text{age}, \text{experience}, \text{literacy}, \text{physical ability}, \text{cognitive style} \,\}
$$

For ASHA workers, typical empirical values from field studies:

$$
\text{Literacy} \;\approx\; \text{Class 8–10}, \quad \text{Visual acuity} \;\approx\; \text{6/9 unaided}, \quad \text{Experience} \;\in\; [1, 15]\;\text{years}
$$

#### 2b — Task Analysis (Hierarchical Task Analysis — HTA excerpt)

```
0. Measure patient SpO₂
   1. Switch on device
      1.1. Press power button (1 s hold)
      1.2. Wait for self-test beep
   2. Position finger on sensor
      2.1. Open clip
      2.2. Insert finger nail-up
      2.3. Close clip
   3. Read display
      3.1. Wait for stable reading (3 s)
      3.2. Record number
   4. Power off
```

#### 2c — Environment

| Environmental Factor | Constraint | Design Implication |
|---|---|---|
| Ambient light | 5–500 lux | High-contrast OLED + backlight |
| Temperature | 15–40 °C | Sensor calibration range |
| Power | Intermittent | 8 h rechargeable battery |
| Cognitive load | Multi-tasking | Single-button operation preferred |

---

### STEP 3 — Specify the User Requirements (Activity 3)

**Conversion Logic:** Translate context-of-use findings into **measurable** requirements.

| ID | Requirement Statement | Usability Attribute | Target Metric |
|---|---|---|---|
| R1 | ASHA worker shall complete measurement in ≤ 15 s | Efficiency | Task time ≤ 15 s |
| R2 | Reading display readable in moonlight (5 lux) | Effectiveness | Contrast ratio ≥ 7:1 |
| R3 | Single-hand operation while wearing gloves | Effectiveness | Task success ≥ 95% |
| R4 | Device shall be cleaned with alcohol wipe | Effectiveness | IP54 sealing |
| R5 | User confidence score after 1 training session | Satisfaction | Likert ≥ 4 / 5 |

**Engineering derivation of R1 — how the 15-second target was reached:**

$$
T_{\text{target}} \;=\; T_{\text{observation}} \times (1 + \alpha_{\text{buffer}})
$$

$$
T_{\text{target}} \;=\; 12\;\text{s observed mean} \;\times\; (1 + 0.25) \;=\; 15\;\text{s}
$$

Where the observed mean $T_{\text{observation}} = 12\;\text{s}$ came from contextual inquiry of current practice, and $\alpha_{\text{buffer}} = 0.25$ is the standard ergonomics safety margin to account for skill variability.

---

### STEP 4 — Produce Design Solutions (Activity 4)

**Conversion Logic:** Move from *low fidelity* to *high fidelity* prototypes.

$$
\text{Design fidelity ladder:}\; \text{Paper sketch} \;\rightarrow\; \text{2D wireframe} \;\rightarrow\; \text{3D printed mock-up} \;\rightarrow\; \text{Functional prototype} \;\rightarrow\; \text{Pilot production}
$$

Three concept variants were generated:

| Concept | C1 — Cuff-style | C2 — Watch-style | C3 — Pendant-style |
|---|---|---|---|
| Single-hand use | Difficult | Easy | Easy |
| Gloved use | OK | Difficult | OK |
| Cleaning | Difficult | Easy | Easy |
| Cost | Low | High | Medium |

**Why we keep 3 concepts, not 1:** Early narrowing eliminates alternatives that may outperform in *later* evaluation criteria.

---

### STEP 5 — Evaluate the Designs Against Requirements (Activity 5)

**Conversion Logic:** Quantitative comparison using ISO 9241-11 metrics.

The 3 concepts were field-tested with 10 ASHA workers, 30 measurements each:

$$
E_{\text{effectiveness, C2}} \;=\; \frac{285}{300} \times 100\% \;=\; 95.0\%
$$

$$
E_{\text{efficiency, C2}} \;=\; \frac{300 \times 13.4\;\text{s}}{285} \;=\; 14.1\;\text{s/task}
$$

$$
\text{SUS, C2} \;=\; 82.5 \quad (\text{above the 68 industry baseline})
$$

**Decision rule:**

$$
\text{Accept design} \;\iff\; E_{\text{eff}} \geq 95\% \;\wedge\; T_{\text{task}} \leq 15\;\text{s} \;\wedge\; \text{SUS} \geq 80
$$

C2 satisfies all three → accepted. C1 and C3 discarded after documented justification.

**Iteration note:** The first C2 prototype failed R3 (gloved use). The thumb-groove was redesigned by 4 mm wider, then re-tested:

$$
\text{Failure rate}_{\text{before}} = 22\%, \quad \text{Failure rate}_{\text{after}} = 4\%
$$

$$
\text{Relative Improvement} = \frac{22 - 4}{22} \times 100\% \approx 81.8\%
$$

This **81.8% improvement** is a typical quantified result KTU examiners expect from a 14-mark answer.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The ISO 9241-210 HCD Iterative Cycle

The following Mermaid block renders the **canonical four-activity iterative cycle** with explicit feedback arrows. This is the most frequently drawn diagram in KTU PEPET744 exam answers.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA([Project Kickoff]) --> planA[Activity 1 Plan the HCD Process]
    planA --> contextA[Activity 2 Understand and Specify Context of Use]
    contextA --> reqA[Activity 3 Specify User Requirements]
    reqA --> designA[Activity 4 Produce Design Solutions]
    designA --> evalA[Activity 5 Evaluate Designs Against Requirements]
    evalA --> decisionA{Usability Goals Met}
    decisionA -- No refine --> designA
    decisionA -- No new requirement --> reqA
    decisionA -- Yes release --> releaseA([System Release])
    style planA fill:#cfe2ff,stroke:#0d6efd
    style contextA fill:#d1e7dd,stroke:#198754
    style reqA fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#ffc107
    style designA fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#dc3545
    style evalA fill:#e2d9f3,stroke:#6610f2
```

---

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Where HCD Sits in System Engineering

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    stakeholderA[Stakeholders and Users] --> reqA2[Requirements Engineering]
    marketA[Market and Regulatory Constraints] --> reqA2
    reqA2 --> hcdA[Human Centred Design Process]
    hcdA --> sysA[Interactive System]
    sysA --> fieldA[Field Deployment]
    fieldA --> hcdA
    sysA --> maintenanceA[Maintenance and Evolution]
    maintenanceA --> hcdA
    style hcdA fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#b8860b,stroke-width:3px
    style sysA fill:#cfe2ff,stroke:#0d6efd
```

---

## 4.3 Mapping the Six HCD Principles to the Four Activities

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    p1[Principle 1 Explicit Understanding] --> contextB[Context of Use]
    p2[Principle 2 Users Involved Throughout] --> reqB[User Requirements]
    p2 --> designB[Design Solutions]
    p2 --> evalB[Evaluation]
    p3[Principle 3 Driven by Evaluation] --> evalB
    p4[Principle 4 Whole User Experience] --> designB
    p5[Principle 5 Multidisciplinary Team] --> planB[Plan HCD Process]
    p6[Principle 6 Process Is Iterative] -.->|feedback loops| evalB
    style p1 fill:#e2d9f3
    style p2 fill:#d1e7dd
    style p3 fill:#fff3cd
    style p4 fill:#f8d7da
    style p5 fill:#cfe2ff
    style p6 fill:#d4edda
```

---

## 4.4 Fidelity Ladder for Design Solutions

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    f1[Paper Sketch Low fidelity] --> f2[Wireframe Mid low fidelity]
    f2 --> f3[Interactive Mockup Mid fidelity]
    f3 --> f4[Functional Prototype High fidelity]
    f4 --> f5[Pilot Production Very high fidelity]
    style f1 fill:#f8d7da
    style f2 fill:#ffe5b4
    style f3 fill:#fff3cd
    style f4 fill:#d1e7dd
    style f5 fill:#cfe2ff
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model QP, PEPET744]`

> Define **Human-Centred Design** as per ISO 9241-210. Mention the **four core activities** of the HCD process.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Human-Centred Design (HCD) is an approach to designing interactive systems that aims to make them **usable** by focusing on the **use of the system** and applying human factors/ergonomics knowledge and techniques (ISO 9241-210).

The four core activities are:

1. **Use and understand the context of use** — identify users, tasks, and environments.
2. **Specify the user requirements** — derive measurable usability goals.
3. **Produce design solutions** — generate alternative prototypes.
4. **Evaluate the designs against requirements** — user-centred testing.

These activities are performed **iteratively** until usability goals are met.

*(Mark split: 1 mark definition, 2 marks activities — awarding as per KTU key.)*

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Expected Pattern, PEPET744]`

> List the **four pillars of usability** defined by ISO 9241-11 and write the formula for **Effectiveness**.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The four pillars of usability (ISO 9241-11) are:

1. **Effectiveness** — accuracy and completeness of goal achievement.
2. **Efficiency** — resources expended relative to accuracy.
3. **Satisfaction** — freedom from discomfort and positive attitude.
4. **Context of use** — the specific users, tasks, equipment, and environment.

Formula for effectiveness:

$$
E_{\text{effectiveness}} \;=\; \frac{\text{Tasks Completed Successfully}}{\text{Tasks Attempted}} \times 100\%
$$

*(Mark split: 2 marks pillars, 1 mark formula.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Question (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

### **Question A — 14 Marks** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024, Expected Pattern, PEPET744]`

> **(a) [7 Marks]** Explain in detail the **four activities** of the Human-Centred Design process as specified by **ISO 9241-210**. Draw the iterative cycle and label all feedback arrows.
>
> **(b) [7 Marks]** Discuss any **four benefits** of adopting Human-Centred Design in interactive systems development. For each benefit, quote one quantitative finding from standard ergonomic literature.

---

#### Model Answer — Part (a) [7 Marks]

**The four activities of HCD according to ISO 9241-210:**

**1. Plan the Human-Centred Design Process.**
The first activity establishes the scope, methods, timelines, and resources for HCD. A written *HCD plan* is produced identifying which user groups will be studied, which usability methods will be used, and the points in the project life-cycle where each will be applied. *(1 mark)*

**2. Understand and Specify the Context of Use.**
This involves empirically gathering data on the *users* (their characteristics), *tasks* (their goals and sub-tasks), and *environments* (physical, social, organisational). Methods include contextual inquiry, interviews, and observations. The output is a documented context-of-use profile. *(1.5 marks)*

**3. Specify the User Requirements.**
Findings from the context analysis are translated into *measurable* user requirements. Each requirement is linked to a usability attribute from ISO 9241-11 (Effectiveness / Efficiency / Satisfaction) with a quantified target value. *(1.5 marks)*

**4. Produce Design Solutions.**
Designers create multiple alternative concepts and iteratively refine them from **low-fidelity sketches** through to **high-fidelity prototypes**. Each iteration is informed by the previous evaluation. *(1.5 marks)*

**5. Evaluate the Designs Against Requirements** *(commonly grouped as a 5th step)*
Usability tests, heuristic evaluations, and field studies are conducted. The results feed back into design refinement. *(1 mark)*

**Iterative cycle diagram (must be drawn in answer sheet):**

```
            ┌──────────────────────────────┐
            │   1. Plan HCD Process        │
            └──────────────┬───────────────┘
                           ▼
            ┌──────────────────────────────┐
            │ 2. Context of Use            │◄────────┐
            └──────────────┬───────────────┘         │
                           ▼                         │
            ┌──────────────────────────────┐         │
            │ 3. Specify User Requirements  │◄────────┤
            └──────────────┬───────────────┘         │
                           ▼                         │
            ┌──────────────────────────────┐         │
            │ 4. Produce Design Solutions   │◄────────┤
            └──────────────┬───────────────┘         │
                           ▼                         │
            ┌──────────────────────────────┐         │
            │ 5. Evaluate Designs           │─────────┘
            └──────────────────────────────┘
```

**Key point for KTU valuation:** All **three feedback arrows** (from Evaluate back to Context, Requirements, and Design) must be visible. *(0.5 mark for diagram)*

---

#### Model Answer — Part (b) [7 Marks]

**Four benefits of HCD with quantitative findings:**

| # | Benefit | Quantitative Finding | Source basis |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Increased user productivity** | Studies report efficiency improvements of **10–30%** | Nielsen 1993; ISO 9241-210 case studies |
| 2 | **Reduced development time and cost** | Fixing a usability defect after release costs up to **100×** more than fixing during design | Boehm / Davis, classic SE literature |
| 3 | **Reduced training and support cost** | Help-desk call volume drops by **30–70%** on HCD-designed systems | IBM / Microsoft case reports cited in HCI textbooks |
| 4 | **Increased user satisfaction** | SUS scores improve by **15–25 points** after iterative redesign | Brooke 1996 SUS, replicated studies |

**Synthesis (closing line for 1 extra mark):**
Adopting HCD therefore shifts cost *leftward* in the project life-cycle — defects are caught in design rather than in field, producing compounding economic benefit.

*(Mark split: 4 × 1.5 marks for each benefit, 1 mark for synthesis / linking back to HCD cycle.)*

---

### **Question B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)**

> **(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **six guiding principles** of Human-Centred Design laid down by ISO 9241-210. Why is each principle non-negotiable in safety-critical interactive systems?
>
> **(b) [7 Marks]** Describe the **Planning the HCD Process** activity in detail. What are the typical contents of a Human-Centred Design Plan document?

---

#### Model Answer — Part (a) [7 Marks]

**The six guiding principles of HCD (ISO 9241-210):**

**P1. Design based on explicit understanding of users, tasks, and environments.**
Without empirical grounding, designers revert to *self-reference*, leading to *design-for-the-designer* failures. In safety-critical systems (e.g., ICU ventilators), unverified assumptions can be lethal. *(1 mark)*

**P2. Users are involved throughout design and development.**
End-users are not just *interviewed once*; they participate in evaluation of every iteration. ASHA workers testing a rural pulse oximeter catch glove-fit issues that lab ergonomists never anticipate. *(1 mark)*

**P3. The design is driven and refined by user-centred evaluation.**
Every redesign decision is anchored in *measured* usability data, not opinion. *(1 mark)*

**P4. The design addresses the whole user experience.**
Task success alone is insufficient. *Trust, anxiety, perceived control, and satisfaction* also matter. *(1 mark)*

**P5. The design team includes multidisciplinary skills and perspectives.**
Ergonomists, software engineers, industrial designers, cognitive psychologists, and domain experts must collaborate. A *single-discipline* team cannot see the system holistically. *(1 mark)*

**P6. The process is iterative.**
Iterative loops are essential because real users reveal requirements that even expert analysis misses. *(1 mark)*

**Why non-negotiable in safety-critical systems:**
In domains like aviation, medical devices, and nuclear control rooms, regulators (FAA, FDA, AERB) **require documented evidence** of P1–P6 application. Skipping any principle violates IEC 62366-1, IEC 60601-1-6, or equivalent safety standards. *(1 mark — synthesis)*

---

#### Model Answer — Part (b) [7 Marks]

**Planning the HCD Process — Detailed Explanation:**

The first activity of HCD, *Planning*, produces a written **HCD Plan** that governs all subsequent activities. It must address six content elements (per ISO 9241-210 Annex A):

| Section | Content | Example from Pulse-Oximeter Case |
|---|---|---|
| **Goals** | Business and usability goals | Reduce misdiagnosis rate by 50% |
| **Target User Groups** | Explicit primary & secondary users | ASHA workers (primary), clinicians (secondary) |
| **Context-of-Use Boundaries** | Where and when the system is used | Rural Kerala, 5–500 lux, monsoon humidity |
| **Methods** | User research and evaluation techniques | Contextual inquiry, SUS, field test |
| **Timing** | When each activity occurs in life-cycle | Plan→Month 1; Field test→Month 4 |
| **Deliverables & Responsibilities** | Who produces what | Ergonomist → user profile; Engineer → prototype |
| **Resources & Budget** | Cost and personnel | ₹15 Lakh, 2 HCD specialists |
| **Risk and Mitigation** | What can derail the plan | User recruitment failure → backup panel |

*(Mark split: 7 marks — distribute 1 mark each for Plan sections and 1 mark for example tying back to case.)*

**Synthesis:** The HCD Plan is a *living document* updated as new risks and findings emerge during the project.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Traps in PEPET744 Module 4 HCD Questions:**
>
> 1. **Drawing the cycle but forgetting feedback arrows.** Without the *back-arrows* from "Evaluate" to earlier activities, the answer is *linear* — you lose **at least 2 marks** in any 14-mark question. **Always draw at least three feedback arrows.**
>
> 2. **Confusing ISO 9241-210 with ISO 9241-11.** 9241-210 is the *process standard*; 9241-11 is the *usability metrics standard*. Examiners **deduct marks** if you interchange them.
>
> 3. **Listing principles without explanation.** "Users are involved" written without *why* and *how* is treated as **half-credit** by KTU valuation keys.
>
> 4. **Skipping the numbers.** When asked about benefits, a *qualitative only* answer scores lower than one with **quantitative figures** (e.g., "10–30% efficiency gain").
>
> 5. **Forgetting ISO 9241-210:2019 date.** Examiners expect the *current revision*; mentioning ISO 13407 (the *superseded* 1999 version) is acceptable but should be acknowledged as superseded.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Human Centred Design Process**

- **Definition:** HCD is an ISO 9241-210 approach that places the *user and context* at the centre of interactive system design.
- **Four Activities (in cycle):** Plan → Context of Use → User Requirements → Design Solutions → Evaluate *(iterate!)*.
- **Six Principles:** Explicit understanding, User involvement, Evaluation-driven, Whole UX, Multidisciplinary team, Iterative.
- **Four Usability Pillars (ISO 9241-11):** Effectiveness, Efficiency, Satisfaction, Context of Use — mnemonically **"E²S + C"**.
- **Key Effectiveness Formula:**
  $$E_{\text{effectiveness}} = \frac{\text{Tasks Completed Successfully}}{\text{Tasks Attempted}} \times 100\%$$
- **Iteration is mandatory** — always draw the feedback arrows in exam diagrams.
- **Multidisciplinary teams** must include ergonomists, not just engineers.
- **Quantitative benefits** to quote in answers: 10–30% productivity, 100× defect-cost multiplier, 30–70% support-cost reduction, 15–25 SUS point gains.
- **Distinguish standards:** ISO 9241-210 (process), ISO 9241-11 (usability metrics), IEC 62366-1 (medical-device usability).
